summary: Centrifuge is an on-chain private credit protocol that enables originators to tokenize real-world assets — trade receivables, invoices, structured loans, and mortgages — as collateral for on-chain financing. Asset originators create isolated credit pools; liquidity providers supply capital and receive pool tokens representing senior (DROP) or junior (TIN) tranches. Centrifuge's largest integrations have been with MakerDAO/Sky Protocol, which allocated significant DAI into Centrifuge pools, and Aave. The protocol operates on Centrifuge Chain (a Substrate-based Polkadot parachain) with Ethereum bridge access. Protocol chain launch date is approximate (mainnet 2021).

postmortem.root_cause: Several early Tinlake pools experienced underwriting failures and delinquencies during 2022 crypto downturn and 2023 SME credit stress. Most notable: ConsolFreight (CF4 pool) and Harbor Trade Credit pools experienced material write-downs. Underlying issue: early Tinlake architecture allowed Asset Originators (AOs) to onboard receivables with limited independent underwriting verification. AOs had operational control over loan origination, valuation, and default declaration — creating principal-agent risk. Junior tranche (TIN) holders absorbed losses as designed, but the cumulative pattern revealed structural underwriting weaknesses.

what_failed.issue: Asset Originators served as both originator and servicer with limited independent oversight. No third-party loan tape verification at pool launch. Default determinations made by AO with delayed transparency to investors.

SME receivables and trade finance assets in early pools lacked diversification and standardized credit scoring. Concentrated exposure to single counterparties within pools (e.g., single shipping company freight receivables).
Early Centrifuge governance lacked formal AO due diligence framework. Pool launches approved by community vote without standardized credit committee or independent rating.

postmortem.outcome: Cumulative write-downs of ~$15M across affected pools (small relative to protocol scale, but material for individual TIN holders). No protocol-level insolvency. Centrifuge subsequently launched Centrifuge V3 with structural improvements: mandatory independent loan tape audits, standardized AO onboarding framework, formal credit committee for new pools, real-time default reporting, and migration to Centrifuge Chain (Substrate) for improved on-chain transparency.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: Centrifuge V1/V2 pools demonstrate the structural risk of vertically-integrated DeFi credit protocols where originator and servicer are the same entity without independent verification. RARM AFI for tokenized credit must enforce L3 separation of duties: independent loan tape verification, independent default declaration mechanism, and independent valuation oracle. Centrifuge V3's redesign is a positive case study — same protocol, materially improved L3+L6 architecture. Demonstrates that RARM scores must be temporally specific (V1 ≠ V3) and that protocol upgrades are legitimate path to improved AFI scores. This is the canonical RWA private-credit lesson and the reason why RARM private-credit indicators emphasize originator/servicer separation.

name: TerraUSD (UST)

custodian.name: N/A — algorithmic, no off-chain reserves at peg

oracle.name: Terra native oracle (validator-reported)

regulator.name: US SEC; South Korea prosecutors; Singapore MAS

summary: Algorithmic stablecoin maintained via mint/burn arbitrage with sister token LUNA. De-pegged May 9, 2022 and collapsed within 72 hours, erasing ~$45B combined market cap. The most consequential stablecoin failure to date and the catalyst for MiCA, GENIUS Act, and HK Stablecoin Ordinance.

sources.title: SEC v. Terraform Labs and Do Kwon (Final Judgment, April 2024)
Nansen: On-chain forensics of UST de-peg

underlying_asset: None at peg. UST was backed by reflexive demand for LUNA. Luna Foundation Guard (LFG) added ~80,000 BTC reserves in Feb-Mar 2022 (~$3B at peak), fully deployed within 48 hours of de-peg without restoring confidence.
reserve_composition: Algorithmic at design (1 UST burnable for $1 of LUNA at oracle price); LFG BTC reserves exhausted during the run.
secondary_market: Previously deep liquidity on Curve (UST-3pool, '4pool'); Anchor Protocol provided 19.5% yield driving 75%+ of UST demand. All venues delisted post-collapse; USTC trades at ~$0.02 on Terra Classic.

smart_contract_audit.auditor: Cosmwasm modules audited piecemeal; no comprehensive audit of full mint/burn + Anchor + LFG system

oracle_provider: Terra validator-set oracle. Reported prices accurately during collapse — the failure was economic, not oracle-level.
redemption_mechanism: Algorithmic: 1 UST burnable for $1 of LUNA at oracle price. Designed assuming LUNA market cap > UST market cap. Failed when LUNA hyperinflated to absorb UST supply.
upgrade_authority: Terraform Labs core team controlled protocol parameters; community governance was nominal as Do Kwon and TFL held effective control.

timeline.event: UST launched on Terra mainnet
Anchor Protocol launches with 19.5% UST yield
Luna Foundation Guard (LFG) established
LFG begins BTC accumulation (~$3B peak)
Large Anchor withdrawals; Curve 3pool imbalance
UST de-pegs to $0.985; LFG deploys BTC reserves
UST falls to $0.60; LUNA hyperinflation begins
Terra blockchain halts; LUNA reaches $0.00001
~$45B combined market cap erased
SEC charges Terraform Labs and Do Kwon with fraud
SEC jury verdict; $4.5B settlement
Do Kwon extradited to United States

postmortem.root_cause: Reflexive algorithmic peg collapsed under sustained sell pressure. UST redemptions minted LUNA at oracle price, hyperinflating LUNA supply from ~350M to 6.5 trillion tokens in 72 hours. The mechanism designed to restore the peg accelerated its destruction. ~75% of UST demand was synthetic (Anchor's 19.5% yield subsidy); LFG's BTC reserves insufficient to absorb the run; Curve 3pool concentration was a single point of liquidity failure.

what_failed.issue: No exogenous collateral at peg. UST was 'backed' by reflexive demand for LUNA — collateral depended on the stablecoin's own success. Late BTC reserves were a band-aid on a fundamentally flawed design.
75%+ of demand driven by Anchor's 19.5% yield, subsidized at $4-7M/day — economically unsustainable. Liquidity concentrated in Curve 3pool; no diversified demand base.
No circuit breaker, no emergency redemption pause, no cap on LUNA minting. Governance nominally decentralized but effectively controlled by Terraform Labs.

SEC established UST and LUNA as unregistered securities; Terraform Labs made materially misleading statements about peg stability and Chai Corporation partnership. $4.5B settlement and criminal extradition followed.

postmortem.outcome: ~$45B combined market cap wiped in 72 hours. Anchor TVL fell from $14B to <$100M. Cascading contagion took down Three Arrows Capital, Celsius, Voyager within 8 weeks. Catalyzed MiCA, GENIUS Act, and HK Stablecoin Ordinance.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: UST is the canonical case for RARM's AFI (Aggregate Fragility Index) layer-gating logic: L2 Asset Quality fell below the failure threshold, so subsequent layers (L3-L6) contribute zero to the aggregate score regardless of nominal quality. No governance, technology, or market structure can compensate for collateral that is reflexive to the token it backs. Secondary lesson: L4 demand-quality flag — sustainable stablecoin demand requires organic, diversified utility, not yield arbitrage from a single source.

underlying_asset: Customer USD deposits converted to UST and deposited into Anchor Protocol. Marketing materials referred to 'USD' and 'stable' without disclosing UST conversion or smart contract risk.
reserve_composition: None held by Stablegains directly. 100% of customer funds routed to Anchor Protocol on Terra blockchain.
secondary_market: N/A — closed system; deposits only withdrawable via Stablegains app.

smart_contract_audit.auditor: Relied on Anchor's audits; no Stablegains-specific audit

timeline.event: Stablegains launches with YC backing
Marketing claims '15% APY on USD savings'; UST exposure not disclosed
UST de-pegs; Stablegains exposure ~$42M of customer funds
Withdrawals suspended; UST conversion losses crystallize
Class action filed in N.D. California
Stablegains announces wind-down
SEC complaint alleges unregistered securities offering

postmortem.root_cause: Stablegains marketed itself as a USD savings product while silently routing 100% of customer deposits into Anchor Protocol's UST yield farm. When UST de-pegged, customers had no recourse: deposits were denominated in collapsing UST, not USD. The company had no reserves of its own, no banking license, no securities registration, and no meaningful disclosure of underlying mechanics. A fintech wrapper that inherited 100% of the wrapped asset's tail risk while presenting none of it to customers.

what_failed.issue: Operated as unregistered securities offering. Marketing 'USD savings' while delivering UST exposure constituted material misrepresentation under federal and California securities law. No banking license, no money transmitter license in most states.
100% counterparty concentration in a single DeFi protocol (Anchor) on a single L1 (Terra). No diversification, no insurance, no segregation of customer funds.
Inherited UST's reflexive algorithmic peg risk while marketing the product as USD-backed. No independent reserve.

postmortem.outcome: ~$42M customer funds lost. Stablegains wound down June 2022. Class action and SEC complaint pending. Cautionary example used in MiCA Article 49 (asset-referenced token disclosure) and US GENIUS Act consumer protection provisions.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: Demonstrates RARM AFI logic at L1: when Legal Foundation falls below threshold (unregistered securities, material misrepresentation), the entire stack scores zero regardless of underlying technology. Also illustrates why RARM treats fintech wrappers as inheriting the AFI of their underlying asset — Stablegains' L2 score was bounded by UST's L2 score, not its own marketing claims. Wrapper products should be evaluated at the consolidated risk level, not the wrapper level.

name: Iron Finance (IRON / TITAN)

custodian.name: N/A — partially-collateralized algorithmic

oracle.name: On-chain TWAP from SushiSwap TITAN/USDC pool

regulator.name: None — operated outside regulatory perimeter

summary: Partially-collateralized algorithmic stablecoin (IRON, $1 peg) backed by USDC + share token TITAN. Suffered first major DeFi 'bank run' on June 16, 2021. TITAN collapsed from $64 to near-zero in hours; IRON de-pegged permanently. Most notable victim: Mark Cuban publicly disclosed losses. Earliest large-scale demonstration of partial-collateral algorithmic stablecoin failure mode.
underlying_asset: IRON was 75% USDC + 25% TITAN at peg. TITAN was a free-floating governance/share token with no underlying asset.
reserve_composition: 75% USDC (exogenous, stable); 25% TITAN (reflexive). Mint/redeem mechanism: 1 IRON minted from $0.75 USDC + $0.25 worth of TITAN burned. Redemption returned same ratio.
secondary_market: TITAN traded on SushiSwap; IRON on QuickSwap/SushiSwap. Liquidity concentrated in 1-2 DEX pools.
oracle_provider: On-chain 10-minute TWAP from SushiSwap TITAN/USDC pool. Single-source oracle with no fallback.
redemption_mechanism: 1 IRON redeemable for $0.75 USDC + $0.25 worth of newly-minted TITAN. Minting TITAN to redeem created death spiral when TITAN price fell.
upgrade_authority: Pseudonymous team multisig; no formal governance until late stage.

timeline.event: Iron Finance launches on BSC
Polygon deployment; TVL reaches $2B by June
Whales begin large IRON redemptions; TITAN sell pressure starts
TWAP oracle lag creates arbitrage death spiral; TITAN $64 → $0.000001 in 4 hours
IRON permanently de-pegs to ~$0.75 (USDC floor)
Mark Cuban publicly discloses losses; calls for stablecoin regulation

postmortem.root_cause: Partial-collateral design (75% USDC + 25% TITAN) created reflexive death spiral when TITAN price fell. Compounding factor: TWAP oracle on SushiSwap had ~10-minute lag, so as TITAN spot price collapsed, the oracle reported stale higher prices. Redemptions arbitraged the lag — minting TITAN at stale high oracle price and dumping at lower spot price, hyperinflating TITAN supply. The mechanism designed to stabilize via arbitrage became the destruction vector.

what_failed.issue: 25% of backing was reflexive (TITAN had no exogenous collateral). Same fundamental flaw as UST but at smaller scale and earlier in time.
Single-source TWAP oracle on a 10-minute lag with no fallback. During fast price moves, oracle lag created risk-free arbitrage that drained the system. No circuit breaker on oracle staleness.
Liquidity concentrated in 1-2 SushiSwap pools. No deep market-making, no diversified venues. Whale redemptions could move TITAN price faster than the oracle could update.

postmortem.outcome: TITAN market cap (~$2B peak) wiped in 4 hours. IRON permanently de-pegged. First widely-publicized partial-algorithmic stablecoin collapse. Mark Cuban's public losses brought retail attention to stablecoin risks 11 months before UST.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: Iron Finance is the L5 Technology canonical case: oracle architecture is a hard constraint on any algorithmic stablecoin design. RARM AFI scoring requires L5 fallback mechanisms (multi-source oracles, staleness checks, circuit breakers) — without them, L5 falls below threshold and the entire stack scores zero. Iron Finance also pre-figured UST: any partial-algorithmic design with reflexive share token will eventually face the same death spiral. The question is not if but when.

name: Tangible Real USD (USDR)

custodian.name: Tangible's UK SPV (real estate); Maker Vault (DAI)

oracle.name: Tangible-reported property valuations

auditor.name: Self-reported reserves; no Big Four audit

regulator.name: None — operated outside regulatory perimeter

summary: Stablecoin backed by tokenized UK rental real estate (TNFT) + DAI + TNGBL share token. First major RWA-backed stablecoin failure: de-pegged October 11, 2023 when DAI liquid reserves (~14.74% of backing) were exhausted by redemptions. Real estate backing (~58%) was illiquid and could not be converted to honor withdrawals. Single most important case study for RWA-backed stablecoin liquidity mismatch.
underlying_asset: Tokenized UK rental properties (~58% at de-peg), DAI (~14.74%), TNGBL governance token (~13%), POL/MATIC liquidity (~14%). Real estate held via UK SPV; tenants paid rent in GBP, converted to crypto yield.
reserve_composition: Mixed: ~58% illiquid tokenized real estate (TNFT) + ~14.74% DAI (liquid) + ~13% TNGBL (reflexive) + remainder in POL liquidity. Liquid backing represented <30% of supply at de-peg.
secondary_market: Curve USDR-am3CRV pool on Polygon; thin liquidity post-incident.

smart_contract_audit.auditor: Unaudited at launch; later partial audits not publicly released

oracle_provider: Tangible-reported property NAV updated quarterly; no independent oracle. Asset valuations subject to issuer discretion.
redemption_mechanism: Direct redemption to DAI at 1:1 while DAI reserves available. Once DAI exhausted, redemption switched to TNFT real estate fractions — illiquid, non-USD denominated, with implicit haircut.
upgrade_authority: Tangible Labs multisig controlled protocol parameters and reserve composition.

timeline.event: USDR launches on Polygon
TVL peaks at ~$45M; rental yield narrative drives growth
DAI reserves drop below 14.74%; large holder redemptions accelerate
DAI reserves exhausted; USDR de-pegs to $0.51 within hours
Tangible announces recovery plan; pivots to TNFT/locker distribution
USDR redemptions paused; recovery process begins
Recovery process ongoing; holders partially repaid in illiquid TNFTs

postmortem.root_cause: Asset-liability liquidity mismatch. USDR claimed daily redeemability against a reserve that was ~58% illiquid real estate. Only DAI portion (~14.74% at de-peg) could honor redemptions on-demand. When redemption demand exceeded DAI liquidity, the system had no mechanism to liquidate real estate quickly — UK property sales take months. Compounded by reflexive TNGBL component (~13%) that lost value as confidence dropped, and oracle dependency on issuer-reported property valuations with no independent verification.

what_failed.issue: Real estate is a legitimate asset class but inherently illiquid. Using it as backing for a daily-redeemable stablecoin created structural maturity mismatch. Additional reflexive TNGBL component (~13%) compounded the problem.
Redemption mechanism promised daily 1:1 to DAI while reserves were 58% illiquid. No tiered redemption schedule, no liquidity buffer minimum, no gate mechanism for orderly wind-down.
UK property SPV concentration; single-jurisdiction tenant base; rental yield denominated in GBP creating FX risk against USD peg. No diversified custody.
Property valuations self-reported by Tangible Labs without independent oracle or third-party appraiser. Quarterly updates created stale-data risk between reporting periods.

postmortem.outcome: USDR de-pegged to $0.51; ~$45M peak supply trapped. Holders partially repaid in illiquid TNFTs and locker tokens. The first significant RWA-backed stablecoin failure. Cited in HKMA stablecoin consultation paper (December 2023) and Bank of England discussion paper on systemic stablecoin requirements as evidence for liquid-reserve minimums.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: USDR is the canonical RWA stablecoin liquidity-mismatch case. RARM AFI for RWA-backed stablecoins must enforce a hard L4 constraint: liquid reserve fraction must equal or exceed maximum plausible daily redemption demand. For real estate backing, this typically means 30-50% liquid buffer or tiered redemption with explicit gates. Secondary lesson: L5 oracle requirements for RWA — issuer-reported NAV is not an oracle. RARM requires independent third-party valuation (Big Four, Knight Frank, or equivalent) at minimum quarterly with monthly attestation between.

summary: Largest regulated fiat-backed stablecoin. De-pegged to $0.87 on March 11, 2023 when $3.3B of reserves were trapped at the failed Silicon Valley Bank. Recovered to peg within 72 hours after FDIC guaranteed all SVB deposits. Included in Lessons Learned as the canonical 'survived major incident' case — demonstrates how L3 Counterparty concentration risk can crystallize even for well-regulated issuers, and how regulatory intervention rather than design alone restored confidence.

sources.title: Circle: USDC reserves and SVB exposure (March 11, 2023 statement)

secondary_market: Deep liquidity on all major CEXs (Binance, Coinbase) and DEXs (Curve 3pool, Uniswap V3). Cross-chain liquidity via CCTP. Daily volume $5-10B.

SVB closed by California regulators; Circle holds $3.3B at SVB
Circle confirms SVB exposure; USDC de-pegs to $0.87
FDIC guarantees all SVB deposits; USDC recovers to $0.99
USDC fully repegs; Circle confirms 100% reserves accessible
Circle obtains EMI license under MiCA (France); first major stablecoin issuer licensed under MiCA
Circle IPO on NYSE (CRCL)

postmortem.root_cause: Pre-SVB reserve management distributed USDC cash reserves across a network of US banks, including $3.3B (~8% of reserves) at SVB. When SVB was closed by California regulators on March 10, 2023, Circle could not access those reserves. Market priced in the worst case (no FDIC recovery beyond $250K limit) and USDC fell to $0.87. The peg was restored not by Circle's design but by federal intervention: Treasury, Fed, and FDIC invoked systemic risk exception to guarantee all SVB deposits. The de-peg lasted ~36 hours.

what_failed.issue: Cash reserve concentration at a single mid-sized bank (SVB ~$3.3B / 8% of reserves). Despite SVB being FDIC-insured, the $250K per-account insurance cap was irrelevant at this scale. Diversification across banks did not eliminate single-bank concentration risk.

DEX liquidity (Curve 3pool) acted as forced price discovery during the de-peg, amplifying panic. Curve 3pool imbalance reached >75% USDC vs DAI/USDT, demonstrating how DEX liquidity concentration becomes contagion vector during incidents.

postmortem.outcome: Peg restored within 72 hours; zero customer losses. Circle subsequently restructured reserves: cash moved primarily to BNY Mellon (G-SIB tier); Treasury allocation increased to ~80% in BlackRock-managed money market fund. Catalyzed Hong Kong Stablecoin Ordinance (June 2025) reserve segregation requirements and MiCA Article 36 reserve diversification rules.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: USDC demonstrates that L3 Counterparty risk is irreducible for fiat-backed stablecoins — even with full regulatory compliance and Big Four attestation, banking system fragility transmits directly to the peg. RARM AFI requires L3 evaluation to consider both regulated-counterparty failure (SVB was solvent on paper) and tail-risk concentration metrics, not just nominal diversification. The 'survived' outcome should not be attributed to USDC's design — it was attributable to government intervention. RARM scoring should treat externally-rescued de-pegs as evidence of latent L3 fragility, not as evidence of design resilience. This case justifies why RARM maintains a 'de_peg_history' risk flag on USDC despite full recovery — historical fragility remains relevant signal.

Not a failure — included as governance lesson for RWA-DeFi integration.

Allocation increased from $500M to $1.25B; controversy on speed of vote

MakerDAO Endgame plan announced; RWA strategy formalized
MakerDAO rebrands to Sky Protocol; DAI continues alongside USDS
Sky Protocol publishes enhanced RWA disclosure framework in response to critiques

postmortem.root_cause: MakerDAO's RWA expansion outpaced its governance and disclosure infrastructure. Monetalis Clydesdale SPV held the largest single concentration of DAI backing ($1.25B at peak) under a structure where: SPV operator (Monetalis) had material control over asset selection within mandate; MKR governance approved high-level mandate but lacked real-time visibility into SPV decisions; no Big Four audit of the SPV (administrator-level attestation only); founder Allan Pedersen's prior advisory relationship with MakerDAO created perceived conflict of interest. The 'failure' here is governance opacity, not asset failure — Treasuries themselves performed as expected.

what_failed.issue: Decision authority concentrated in Monetalis operator with limited MKR holder oversight. Material SPV changes governed by MIP process with 7-day delay, but day-to-day operations opaque. Conflicts of interest disclosed but not structurally mitigated.

Cayman SPV structure provides asset isolation but limited recourse for MKR holders. No clear fiduciary duty from Monetalis to MKR token holders established under Cayman law. Bankruptcy-remoteness untested.

Single-operator concentration (Monetalis) for $1.25B exposure. Administrator-level attestation (Ankura Trust) rather than Big Four audit. Custodian diversification adequate but operator diversification absent.

postmortem.outcome: No financial losses; assets performed as expected. Reputational and governance critique catalyzed Sky Protocol's enhanced disclosure framework (February 2025) and contributed to MakerDAO Endgame restructuring. Used as case study in MAS Project Guardian working papers on DeFi-RWA governance integration.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: Monetalis illustrates that asset-level RARM scoring is insufficient for hybrid DeFi-RWA structures. RARM AFI must evaluate the full chain: token holder → DAO governance → off-chain operator → SPV → custodian → underlying asset. Each link is a potential failure point. L6 Governance scoring for DeFi-RWA structures should require: (1) independent operator audit, not just administrator attestation; (2) real-time NAV oracle, not monthly attestation; (3) structural conflict-of-interest mitigation, not disclosure alone; (4) MKR-equivalent holder fiduciary duty established in operating jurisdiction. Without these, L6 falls below threshold and downstream L2-L5 scoring cannot compensate.

Initially celebrated as RWA breakthrough but secondary market for property NFTs failed to materialize. PRO governance token fell ~95% from 2022 peak. Currently operates primarily as traditional real estate transaction platform; NFT minting paused for new properties. Lessons Learned case study for tokenization-without-liquidity failure mode.

timeline.event: Propy ICO; PRO token launched
First US home sold as NFT (Tampa, FL) — ~$650K
PRO token reaches peak ~$4.50; ~20 properties tokenized

PRO token down ~95% from peak; platform focus shifts to AI title insurance

postmortem.root_cause: Single-property NFTs failed to generate secondary market demand for structural reasons that Propy could not solve through tokenization alone: (1) Real estate transactions involve due diligence (inspection, title search, financing) that cannot be replicated in a click-to-buy NFT flow; (2) US property law requires recordation with county clerks — NFT transfer alone does not perfect title without parallel deed recording; (3) Mortgage financing for tokenized property unavailable from any major US lender; (4) Tax treatment of LLC-membership NFTs ambiguous (capital gains? real estate? collectible?). The NFT format added friction without removing the underlying transaction complexity. Demand evaporated once novelty wore off.

what_failed.issue: Single-property NFTs are non-fungible by definition — each property is unique with its own legal, financial, and physical characteristics. No buyer pool develops for an asset where every unit requires custom underwriting. Tokenization without fungibility achieves no liquidity benefit.

US property law requires county-level deed recording for legal title transfer. NFT transfer creates LLC membership transfer but does not perfect underlying real property title without parallel off-chain process. Legal effectiveness untested at scale; mortgage lenders refused to accept tokenized title as collateral.

Real estate is a legitimate asset class but unsuited to whole-property NFT format. Fractionalization (REIT-style) addresses fungibility but requires different legal structure (securities offering). Propy's whole-NFT model fell between two stools.

postmortem.outcome: Propy NFT business effectively wound down; ~20 tokenized properties remain in legacy state. PRO token down ~95% from peak. Company pivoted to AI-powered title insurance and traditional transaction platform. Real estate NFT category broadly stalled — subsequent projects (Lofty.ai, Roofstock onChain) moved to fractionalized model.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: Propy is the canonical L4 case for RWA tokenization: tokenization does not create liquidity, fungibility does. RARM AFI for real estate must distinguish between (a) whole-asset NFTs (no fungibility, no liquidity premium); (b) fractionalized tokens with fungible exposure to a single property (limited fungibility within property); (c) pool-based fractionalization (true fungibility across diversified property pool, REIT-equivalent). Only (c) achieves meaningful L4 liquidity scoring. Secondary lesson at L1: tokenized real estate requires explicit legal framework integration with local property registry — Singapore Project Orchid and Hong Kong DLT property registry pilots are responses to this. Whole-property NFT without registry integration is structurally fragile.

timeline.event: Maple Finance V1 launched on Ethereum
Orthogonal Trading default ~$31M (FTX exposure)
Maple V2 launched with overcollateralized lending
Solana expansion; syrupUSDC reaches $200M+ TVL

postmortem.root_cause: Maple V1 used undercollateralized lending: pool delegates underwrote crypto-native institutions (market makers, prop trading firms) based on KYC and creditworthiness, not collateral. Following FTX collapse in November 2022, Orthogonal Trading (a Maple pool delegate AND borrower) concealed FTX exposure losses while continuing to draw from pools it managed. Auros also defaulted due to FTX losses. Cumulative ~$36M write-downs across pools.

what_failed.issue: Same entity (Orthogonal) served as pool delegate (underwriter) and borrower — fundamental conflict of interest. KYC-based credit underwriting could not detect concealed exposure to FTX.
Undercollateralized loans to crypto-native firms; concentration in entities with FTX exposure. No diversification across uncorrelated counterparties.
No protocol-level circuit breaker for delegate-conflict situations; default declaration controlled by pool delegate.

postmortem.outcome: ~$36M write-downs across affected pools (Orthogonal, Auros). Maple subsequently launched V2 with overcollateralized lending (Syrup) and stricter delegate separation. Demonstrates the institutional private-credit lesson: vertical integration of underwriting and borrowing without independent verification creates structural fragility.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: Maple V1 → V2 transition is a positive case for RARM AFI temporal scoring: same protocol, materially improved L3 architecture (separation of underwriter from borrower, overcollateralization). RARM AFI for tokenized credit should require: (1) independent verification of pool delegate exposure; (2) overcollateralization or independent insurance for undercollateralized exposure; (3) protocol-level default declaration not controlled by interested parties. V1 fell below L3 threshold; V2 architecturally addresses these constraints.

summary: First major music royalty NFT platform. Founded by DJ Justin '3LAU' Blau and Opendoor co-founder JD Ross; raised $71M (a16z, Paradigm, Coinbase Ventures). Sold LDAs (Limited Digital Assets) representing fractional streaming royalties for tracks by Nas, Diplo, The Chainsmokers, Logic. Platform investing business shut down in 2024 due to disappointing royalty payouts (e.g., one Royce da 5'9' drop went 18+ months without payments), shifting regulatory landscape, and questions about long-term economic viability of small-track royalty fractionalization. Existing LDAs remain claimable via legacy site. Important case study in early IP/royalty RWA fragility.

reserve_composition: No reserve — payments depend on artist actually remitting royalties received from DSPs to Royal's treasury contract.
secondary_market: Originally Royal's marketplace + OpenSea; marketplace sunset 2024; OpenSea trading still possible but limited liquidity.
oracle_provider: N/A — off-chain royalty reporting from DSPs.
redemption_mechanism: No principal redemption. Holders earn quarterly royalty distributions for duration of rights, claimable via treasury contract.

timeline.event: Royal founded by 3LAU and JD Ross
$55M Series A led by a16z
Nas drops 'Ultra Black' and 'Rare' — first major artist sale, $560K gross

postmortem.root_cause: Royal's model required artists to remit royalty payments received from DSPs to Royal's treasury for distribution to LDA holders. This created two structural fragilities: (1) Reliance on artist compliance — when payments lagged or stopped (e.g., Royce da 5'9' drop), Royal had limited enforcement mechanism; (2) Economic viability — streaming royalties for individual tracks are typically very small (~$0.003 per stream), meaning fractional LDA holders received tiny payouts that frequently didn't justify gas fees or platform overhead. Combined with crypto bear market reducing speculative demand for music NFTs, the unit economics collapsed.

what_failed.issue: Royal had no custody over the underlying royalty stream — it depended on the artist receiving payment from DSPs and voluntarily remitting share to Royal's treasury. No legal mechanism comparable to publishing rights administration; effectively unsecured promise from artist.
Single-track fractionalization produced LDAs with unique, small, illiquid cashflows. Each LDA was effectively a unique micro-bond with no comparable trading peer, killing secondary market liquidity.

Reg A / Reg D offerings did not provide investor protections comparable to publishing royalty trusts or music funds (Hipgnosis-style). LDA classification under US securities law remained ambiguous; KYC whitelist did not address fundamental enforceability questions.

postmortem.outcome: Platform investing business effectively closed April 2024. ~$71M VC capital largely written down. Holders of LDAs from 2021-2022 drops retain claims but receive minimal distributions. Subsequent music IP tokenization platforms (Anotherblock, JKBX) pivoted to acquiring rights upfront rather than relying on artist remittance — direct lesson from Royal's failure mode.
postmortem.rarm_lesson: Royal demonstrates RARM AFI logic at L3: when counterparty enforcement depends on voluntary remittance with no legal mechanism for capture-at-source, L3 falls below threshold regardless of how good the on-chain infrastructure is. RARM AFI for IP/royalty tokenization should require: (1) rights acquired or assigned at source, not promised by ongoing artist remittance; (2) administration via established music publishing/PRO infrastructure rather than novel platform-administered treasury; (3) unit economics check — minimum cashflow per LDA must justify distribution overhead. Anotherblock's success vs Royal's failure illustrates that the same asset class (music royalties) can be tokenized well or badly depending on whether L3 (counterparty enforcement) is structurally sound.

summary: Franklin Templeton's flagship tokenized US government money market fund — distinct from BENJI as a retail-accessible 1940 Act-registered fund (BENJI is the institutional ticker). As of mid-2025 had ~$750M AUM tokenized exposure across chains. summary: First tokenized money market fund issued by a Chinese asset management subsidiary in Hong Kong. Launched February 2024 under HK SFC's tokenization circular framework (Nov 2023). Marks the entry of mainland Chinese asset management capability into HK's tokenization ecosystem. Targets professional investors (PIs) including Asian institutional capital seeking yield-bearing tokenized exposure outside mainland regulatory constraints. Strategic importance: HK as the regulated bridge between mainland Chinese asset management capability and global tokenization infrastructure — a central pillar of HK's competitive positioning vs Singapore.
